reply_content: In Korean society, showing respect for elders is paramount. This tradition reflects the Confucian emphasis on a hierarchical social structure and the honor due to those who are older and wiser. When a younger person backs away from an elder, they are deliberately maintaining eye contact and demonstrating that their full attention and deference are on the elder until the very end of the interaction. Turning your back would be considered highly disrespectful, a non-verbal sign of dismissal. This gesture is a powerful, non-verbal way of saying, "You are so important that I will not take my eyes off you."

comment_content:
Someone expressed "K/Da" as a short form for Korean Drama — I think that's such a creative expression. And what I want to introduce is the characteristics of the tiger in the Joseon Dynasty.
In the late Joseon period, as royal court paintings transitioned into folk paintings (minhwa), the tiger (Derpy)began to be depicted as a foolish character alongside a proud magpie(Sussie) often used to satirize society.
Although tigers were feared for attacking and eating people—leading to tiger hunts—they were also seen as “san-gun,” or "mountain Lords" believed to protect villages.
reply_content: That's awesome. Thank you for explanation.
